---
title: 'Class Discussion Posts: The Law, Torah, and Grace... Luther was clueless, but he meant well.'
date: '2022-03-20T00:42:00.0040000+00:00'
draft: false
blogger_blog: 'NoHiding.Faith'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '2442740569451266000'
blogger_filename: '/2022/03/class-discussion-posts-law-torah-and.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/NoHiding.Faith/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Ancient Near East (ANE)'
  - 'Class Discussion Posts'
---

<div style="text-align: center;"><br /></div><p style="text-align: left;">Based on a recent conversation, I wanted to re-post an answer I gave because I think it sums up much of the deconstruction I've had around "Judaism" and "The Law". <br /></p><p style="text-align: left;"></p><p style="text-align: left;">My classmate said, "We are called to obedience not out of "have to" but out of love for God and his teaching."&nbsp;</p><p style="text-align: left;">To which I replied: Yes! And so was everyone in the biblical narrative, from Abraham to David to Jesus to Paul. </p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>"Rabbis and other Jewish scholars protested for centuries that neither of these propositions was true. “How can you say we believe in salvation by works?” they asked. “We say a little Jewish boy at eight days old is brought into the covenant by circumcision. What could he have done to deserve that? And why did God choose Israel? Perhaps the merits of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob had something to do with it, but how do we millions of Jews join the covenant that God made with them? The same way as that little boy, before we have done anything to merit it. It is by what you call grace." -- Gerald R. McDermott, <a href="https://amzn.to/3N95TrE" target="_blank">Israel Matters: Why Christians Must Think Differently about the People and the Land</a> (Grand Rapids, MI: Brazos Press: A Division of Baker Publishing Group, 2017), 56.<i> (Affiliate Link)</i><br /></li></ul> 
<div style="text-align: center;"><iframe frameborder="0" marginheight="0" marginwidth="0" scrolling="no" src="//ws-na.amazon-adsystem.com/widgets/q?ServiceVersion=20070822&amp;OneJS=1&amp;Operation=GetAdHtml&amp;MarketPlace=US&amp;source=ss&amp;ref=as_ss_li_til&amp;ad_type=product_link&amp;tracking_id=toposcreative-20&amp;language=en_US&amp;marketplace=amazon&amp;region=US&amp;placement=1587433958&amp;asins=1587433958&amp;linkId=8ef1195a8755b5970f68086f6e521423&amp;show_border=true&amp;link_opens_in_new_window=true" style="height: 240px; width: 120px;"></iframe></div>

 <p style="text-align: left;"><b>Maybe this can help reframe it:</b></p> 
 <ul style="text-align: left;"><li>If you define "law" (a bad English mistranslation of the word 
Torah) as "the stuff I have to do", then you are using Luther's lenses, 
not the biblical author's lenses. </li><ul><li>Luther was reacting to medieval Catholicism. In his day and age, 
cold, dead, religious have-to practices permeated the atmosphere. So 
when Luther reacted, he was reacting not to the ancient Israelite 
covenant but to medieval Catholicism. </li></ul><li>Torah was given to Israel AFTER they were already chosen, rescued,
 and adopted without earning a dime of it. They existed then (and exist 
today) purely by the Grace and Love of Yahweh. </li><li>Torah is about loving God back, not about keeping a set of "rules". </li><li>The Torah (Teachings/Instructions) is primarily composed of five 
books (Genesis to Deuteronomy). It can also be short-hand for the entire
 Tanakh (Torah, Prophets, and Writings). 
   <ul><li>Primarily, Torah is a Theological Historical Narrative, mixed with Hebrew Poetry, and Ancient Near East (ANE) Cosmologies and Origin Stories which serve as Polemics (stories used as arguments against) to other ANE culture origin stories... and yes, it has a few "laws" in it too. <br /></li><li>Within the Torah proper, mostly in Leviticus but also sprinkled 
throughout the other books, there are 611 law codes (the number spells 
Torah in Hebrew numerals). These laws are most likely not every law that
 existed in ancient Israel or every law that Moses and successors wrote 
down. These laws are the ones preserved for us by those who finalized 
the cannon, which the Holy Spirit led them to include for our meditation
 and wisdom. 
     <ul><li>Example: "When you build a new house, make a parapet around 
your roof". In the Ancient Near East (ANE), a parapet was a small wall 
around the roof. People used their roof as a living room, guests came 
there for dinner and hanging-out time. This short roof-wall kept people 
from accidentally falling off. It was a way to preserve life.&nbsp;</li><li>When you build a new house, make a parapet around your roof so that you 
may not bring the guilt of bloodshed on your house if someone falls from
 the roof. Deuteronomy 22:8 <a href="https://biblehub.com/deuteronomy/22-8.htm" target="_blank">https://biblehub.com/deuteronomy/22-8.htm</a> &nbsp; </li><li>Today: We don't use our roof for a living space (usually). 
However, we do add staircase handrails to our two-story homes and 
buildings. This is much the same wisdom Torah is asking us to consider 
when we live our lives.<br /> 
       <ul><li>Though it sounds awesome, and one house local does have 
this I should get a picture. Go to street view on GoogleMaps for this 
address: 811 N Government Way Coeur d'Alene, Idaho&nbsp; </li></ul> </li></ul> </li><li>While Torah lists Blessings and Curses, they are simply the 
state of affairs. The world is in Chaos. Yahweh is the anti-Chaos agent.
 If you want Order (blessing, fruitfulness) you will do things the way 
Yahweh has asked you to. If you step outside of his ways, Chaos reigns. </li><li>Throughout Ancient Israel's existence, Yahweh was overly 
gracious, patient, kind, offered a myriad of chances, and only as a last
 and final resort, brought in a series of exiles to purge their 
adultery. </li></ul> </li><li>Contexts Matter: The ancient Israelite was just that, an ancient 
Semitic group of individuals living within a particular context. Yahweh 
communicated with them within that context. When you look at other 
Ancient Near East (ANE) law codes (like the <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Code_of_Hammurabi" target="_blank">Code of Hammurabi</a>), what you
 find is remarkably similar rules and laws. However, what you also find 
is Yahweh upping the anti by moving, progressing Israel forward within 
their own Ancient Near East context as compared to their neighbors. Many
 of the laws and rules for Ancient Israel are irrelevant today, even to 
modern Orthodox Jews. However, each contains some wisdom within their 
ANE culture that can be extrapolated and applied today. Like the 
parapets mentioned above. </li><li>During the return from exile, Ezra and Nehemiah found themselves 
in a mixed bag of returning exiles. Many of them had intermarried, some 
of them had lost the Hebrew language. Nehemiah at one point tears a 
man's hair out for this and commands them to divorce their newly found 
spouses/kids. 
   <ul><li>Note: Nowhere in the text does the Bible indicate that Yahweh 
endorses or approves of Nehemiah's command, it simply records that it 
happened. </li></ul> </li><li>These two returning exiles establish a group of scribes hoping to 
get the people of God to follow Yahweh's rule in hopes of preventing 
another exile and usher in the Day of Yahweh (Day of the Lord), in which
 Messiah will come. </li><li>These scribes eventually, through 300+years become the "Scribes 
and Pharisees" who minister within Israel, hoping to purify Israel and 
looking forward to Messiah. </li><li>During these years, they "built a hedge around Torah", and the 
practice of Second Temple Judaism became more legalistic in its 
Orthopraxy (practices). They figured, "we can't violate it if we don't 
even get close to it". So whatever they imagined the "rule" was, they 
tried to make new rules to keep people distant from the Torah rule. This
 is the legalism Yeshua and Paul were reacting to; not to Torah itself 
but the way Torah was practiced in the Second Temple Period by specific 
sects of Judaism. </li><li>Contrary to popular Christian understanding, many Pharisees came 
to believe in Yeshua as Messiah, the one they looked for. Some did not. 
The Temple Leadership (primarily Sadducees, but also containing some 
Pharisees) ultimately executed Yeshua, but that was not a unanimous 
decision. </li><li>Yeshua (and later Paul (Greek name)/Saul (Hebrew name)) debated 
the Pharisees, not as outsiders but insiders. Yeshua was closest in 
style to the Pharisees. They each wanted to live Torah well, and Yeshua 
was attempting to get them to see that in trying to live Torah, they 
actually violated it. 
   <ul><li>You say... but I say... </li><li>Yeshua's Mishnah was one of the heart, rather than actions. </li></ul> </li><li>So, while the Second Writings (aka New Testament) have examples of
 attitudes that are anti-legalisticness... These are reactions to the 
attitudes and practices that developed during the post-biblical Second 
Temple Period; not, to the Torah itself. </li></ul> 
 <p style="text-align: left;">I hope that at least gives you something to chew on.</p><div style="text-align: center;"><iframe frameborder="0" marginheight="0" marginwidth="0" scrolling="no" src="//ws-na.amazon-adsystem.com/widgets/q?ServiceVersion=20070822&amp;OneJS=1&amp;Operation=GetAdHtml&amp;MarketPlace=US&amp;source=ss&amp;ref=as_ss_li_til&amp;ad_type=product_link&amp;tracking_id=toposcreative-20&amp;language=en_US&amp;marketplace=amazon&amp;region=US&amp;placement=1587433958&amp;asins=1587433958&amp;linkId=8ef1195a8755b5970f68086f6e521423&amp;show_border=true&amp;link_opens_in_new_window=true" style="height: 240px; width: 120px;"></iframe></div>
